common_models.name: Geometric circle-covering model (Kershner 1939 hexagonal covering) — reducing 'water the whole field' to covering a rectangle with the fewest equal-radius circles

Constraint-driven effective-radius calculation — shrinking the throw radius from 13.43 m (where rate=0) to an effective 11.5 m needed to deliver 2 cm in 5 hours, then shifting the left row inward by 1.93 m to keep sprinklers in bounds
Simultaneous Perturbation Stochastic Approximation (SPSA) — stochastic gradient optimizer minimizing the standard deviation of accumulated precipitation across the field grid (maximizing uniformity)
Discrete grid (matrix-of-cells) Monte-Carlo-style irrigation simulator in MATLAB — accumulates precipitation per 0.25 m cell from each sprinkler
Cubic spline interpolation of the measured sprinkler application-rate-vs-distance profile (Nelson WF16 #16 nozzle data from CIT)
Hazen-Williams pipe pressure-loss equation and the nozzle-discharge equation (Q = 29.82·sqrt(P)·D^2·Cd) for hydraulic feasibility checks
Greedy nearest-neighbor pipe-setup minimization heuristic — lateral-distance sorting with a 20 m pipe-length and 0.75 cm/hr overlap-rate feasibility test
Coefficient of Uniformity (CU = 1 - std/(1+std)) as the scalar objective/scoring metric

Bernoulli equation + continuity (mass conservation) to derive nozzle exit velocity (~28.9 m/s), per-nozzle discharge (~48.35 L/min), and the cap of 3 nozzles before pressure drops below the Rain Bird 280 kPa minimum
Empirical 'sprinkler constant' Cs ~ 0.983 fit from Rain Bird discharge-vs-pressure data to capture friction loss in the discharge relation Q = Cs*Ac*sqrt(2P/rho)

Projectile motion with linear (Stokes) air drag: coupled ODEs dvx/dt = -k*vx, dvy/dt = -g - k*vy, solved in closed form, with drag constant k=1.20314 calibrated numerically so the jet range matches the manufacturer's 19.5 m radius

Two competing radial water-distribution models for a single rotating sprinkler: (a) Triangular Distribution (application rate falls linearly with distance, a 3-D cone) and (b) Exponential Decay Distribution f(r) = (3*0.00267*e^(-0.7r) + 0.00267)*e^(-(r/19.5)^20) fit to Louie & Selker experimental data, rotated about the axis via r = sqrt((x-a)^2+(y-b)^2) to place a sprinkler at (a,b)

Lattice superposition model: total application = sum of individual sprinkler distributions over triangular and rectangular (square) grids; optimized spacing-to-radius ratio for uniformity (square at 1.1*radius, triangular lattice at 0.85*radius)
A uniformity objective: Aver(D,R) = (1/Area)∫_R D, and a variance-like Std(D,R) = (1/Area)∫_R (D - Aver)^2 over a unit cell of the lattice, plus Max-Min spread, used to score configurations

Combinatorial/geometric coverage argument: a lower bound of M_T = 2 pipe moves from the fact that two points >59m apart (19.5+20+19.5) cannot be covered at once; enumeration of layouts by number of moves M_T = 2,3,4 and a full-field numerical grid evaluation on [0,80]x[0,30]

common_techniques.name: Decomposition of a coupled optimization into a pipeline of sub-problems: (1) full coverage via circle-covering, (2) boundary adjustment, (3) uniformity via SPSA, (4) pipe-setup minimization via greedy heuristic, (5) hydraulic verification — each stage feeds the next
Using a closed-form geometric optimum (Kershner's proven-optimal hexagonal covering) as the initialization/blueprint, then refining numerically rather than optimizing blindly from random starts

Cross-validation between two independent methods: the analytic shifted-covering layout and the SPSA numerical optimizer converge to nearly the same configuration (CU 0.89 vs 0.90), used as mutual confirmation
Translating physical delivery constraints (2 cm in available time, 0.75 cm/hr cap) into geometric spacing constraints (effective radius 11.5 m, minimum 21 m sprinkler separation)

Profile substitution with explicit scaling: no data existed for the 0.6 cm nozzle, so they took the documented 1/8" Nelson profile and scaled the application rate by ~3.3x from the flow-rate ratio (12.57/3.42 gpm)
Showing optimizer convergence visually across iteration snapshots (random -> 100 -> 500 -> 5000 iterations) to demonstrate the SPSA is actually working

Order-of-magnitude pruning of negligible effects: computing mainline (0.0122 psi) and riser (0.058 psi) pressure losses and explicitly discarding them as negligible to justify the constant-pressure assumption
Three-stage decomposition (physical characterization -> evaluation metric -> configuration search) that cleanly separates 'how the hardware behaves' from 'how to deploy it'

Anchoring an idealized derivation to a real commercial product (Rain Bird 70H 1" sprinkler) and its published spec sheet, so derived numbers (radius, discharge, pressure floor) are cross-checked against manufacturer data rather than invented
Calibrating free parameters against ground truth: the drag constant k and the sprinkler constant Cs are both back-solved so the model reproduces the known 19.5 m radius and measured discharge

Deriving a hard scheduling constraint from physics: because the exponential model peaks near ~1 cm/hr at the head (>0.75 cm/hr limit), sprinklers can run at most ~45 min/hr, which then propagates into total operating-hours and water-use calculations

Reducing the design space with a provable lower bound (M_T >= 2 moves) and then climbing M_T = 2 -> 3 -> 4 to expose the uniformity/stability/time trade-off, showing 3 moves is dominated and 4 is the sweet spot

Explicitly modeling 'stability' as robustness to wind/pressure perturbation via d_max (max distance from any field point to nearest sprinkler) and tolerance bands (e.g. Creative Farmer tolerates ~25% deviation, Conservative ~7m shift)

Two-model sensitivity hedge: testing every layout under both the optimistic Triangular and the pessimistic Exponential distribution, arguing any scheme acceptable under exponential is safe under triangular

Quantitative final scorecard: each candidate (Lazy, Passive-Aggressive, Conservative, Creative) reported with mean, variance, min, max application rate plus moves, hours of operation, and total liters used

recurring_pitfalls.name: The core sprinkler profile is borrowed from a 1/8" (#16) Nelson nozzle, half the diameter of the problem's 0.6 cm (~1/4") nozzle, then rescaled by a flow-rate factor (~3.3x) with no guarantee the spatial shape stays the same — the entire model rests on an unvalidated input the team themselves flag

SPSA was never actually run to convergence: they admit they couldn't reach the 5000th-iteration coordinate output due to compute limits and instead ASSUME the analytic shifted-covering layout approximates it. The headline cross-validation (0.89 vs 0.90) is therefore partly assumed rather than fully demonstrated

Coefficient of Uniformity is an averaged scalar — as the team notes, very different (even pathological) spatial patterns can share the same CU, so CU=0.89 does not by itself prove good spatial uniformity, and they care asymmetrically about under- vs over-watering but the metric is symmetric
No-wind assumption is a major simplification: wind reshapes the throw profile (shortens upwind, elongates downwind) and could invalidate the whole circular-coverage premise

The appendix code is buggy/unfinished — typos (thetammin/thetamin, sprinkler_posistions), the perturbation_algorithm.m feasibility branches have empty bodies, and there are unit inconsistencies (the text once says '75 cm per hour' and '420 Pascals' where kPa/0.75 cm-hr were meant) — sloppiness that undercuts the reproducibility claim

Each pipe set ends up containing only ONE sprinkler (because 21 m min spacing exceeds the 20 m pipe length), so the elaborate multi-sprinkler pipe-fitting machinery effectively collapses to a trivial case — the modeling sophistication exceeds what the final answer required
Zero real-world or sensitivity validation: all verification is in-simulation, with no robustness analysis over nozzle profile, spacing, or pressure

Several equations are typeset/printed incorrectly, which a stricter judge would flag: the standard-deviation formula is actually a variance (no square root, and missing 1/Area normalization consistency), and the discharge constant line writes sqrt(2/(rho*P)) where the derivation needs sqrt(2P/rho)

The initial-condition v0 = 1 m for the drag ODE is almost certainly a typo (should be ~28.5 m/s exit velocity); using v0=1 would not give a 19.5 m range, so the drag constant k=1.20314 is fit to a possibly inconsistent setup

The 'Conservative Farmer' and 'Creative Farmer' algorithm tables in Section 7 are identical (both list pipes at x=10,30,50,70) even though one is described as a square grid and the other as an equilateral-triangle grid, so the reproducibility of the triangular layout is broken in the writeup
Wind is never modeled mechanistically; it enters only as a tolerance band on coverage distance (d_max), so 'stability' ratings are heuristic rather than simulated

Major resource inefficiency is under-examined: the Lazy Farmer uses 113,000 L vs. a 48,000 L theoretical optimum (42% water efficiency), and 4-move schemes use 226,000 L, yet water cost is not folded into the final ranking despite the problem emphasizing avoiding over-watering and conserving a 'precious resource'
Single hardware instance: the whole analysis is pinned to one Rain Bird 70H model; the claim that other manufacturers give similar radii is asserted with little supporting data
Evaporation, soil infiltration/runoff, and crop-specific sensitivity (all raised in the problem) are largely assumed away (flat 5% evaporation, uniform soil) rather than modeled
The exponential fit f(r) mixes variable names (uses x in the formula but r=distance), and its calibration to the larger sprinkler via ad hoc width/height scaling is only loosely justified

reusable_patterns.name: Reduce-to-a-known-problem: map the messy modeling task onto a classical solved problem (here, circle covering) so you inherit a proven-optimal baseline and a citation, then perturb to handle real constraints
Initialize a numerical optimizer from an analytic optimum instead of random restarts — faster convergence and a sanity anchor
Triangulate the answer with two independent methods and report their agreement as evidence of correctness (analytic layout vs SPSA), not just a single pipeline output
Convert physical/operational constraints into geometric ones early (delivery target + rate cap -> effective radius -> minimum spacing) so the optimization works in clean spatial variables
When required data is missing, find the closest real measured data, state the gap explicitly, and apply a defensible scaling law (flow-rate ratio) rather than fabricating a function
Always run a hydraulic/physical feasibility check and explicitly discard negligible terms with numbers (e.g. pressure losses of 0.0122 and 0.058 psi) to justify simplifying assumptions
Deliver the answer twice: once as a labeled figure of the optimized layout, once as an operator-ready table with wall-clock times and exact coordinates

Use a two-tier assumptions list (load-bearing vs operational), each with a one-line justification, and pair it with a specific, technically literate Weaknesses section that names concrete failure modes
Show optimizer convergence as a sequence of iteration snapshots to prove the algorithm is doing real work
Adopt a single scalar scoring metric (Coefficient of Uniformity) as the optimization objective, but explicitly discuss its blind spots so the judge trusts your judgment

Three-layer pipeline pattern for any physical-system design problem: (1) derive/characterize the device from first principles, (2) define a single scalar quality metric, (3) search the deployment/configuration space against that metric — keeps physics, scoring, and optimization decoupled

Calibrate-to-reality pattern: pick a real commercial component, pull its published spec sheet, and back-solve any free constants (friction Cs, drag k) so your idealized model reproduces a known quantity — instantly buys credibility and a validation check
Derive operational constraints from the physics, not just from the prompt: here the per-head peak rate forced a 45-min/hr duty cycle that cascaded into hours-of-operation and water-budget numbers

Lower-bound-then-climb search: prove a combinatorial/geometric lower bound (min moves), then increment the discrete decision variable to map out the trade-off frontier instead of brute-forcing everything
Unit-cell variance as a uniformity metric: evaluate Aver/Std/Max/Min over one repeating lattice cell (superposition of overlapping kernels) to compare periodic spatial patterns cheaply
